Staff at IndyGo have recommended the agency's board authorize a one‑year contract extension for Ecolane
column5: the paratransit operations technology platform for IndyGo Access. The proposed extension
column6: valued at a not‑to‑exceed amount of $228
column8: is intended to run from August 2025 through August 2026 and was prompted by a desire to postpone a new technology RFP until after several major operational transitions are completed.

column1: Operational Stability Prioritized Over Immediate Technology Procurement
IndyGo's Deputy Chief Transportation Officer
column2: Mike Broad
column3: stated that the Ecolane platform is currently used by mobility staff and contractors for its paratransit services. The staff recommendation to delay a new technology RFP stems from several significant
column4: simultaneous operational changes. These include the onboarding of a new paratransit contractor
column5: MV Transportation
column6: and a new care‑center provider. The rationale is to allow staff to complete training and stabilize the service under the new operating structure before undertaking a major technology procurement.

column1: The one-year extension ensures continuity for critical passenger-facing features. According to Broad
column2: the extension includes the mobile app features that customers may opt into for real‑time pickup windows and estimated arrival at their destination. Call‑center agents will also continue to provide the customer pickup window when booking trips. The software licensing renewal will be funded from the local operating budget. Staff described the procurement as a special procurement for which DBE participation was unavailable. While the recommendation was presented to the board with discussion and questions
column3: the provided source does not record a final vote.

The decision by IndyGo staff reflects a larger industry trend where transit agencies prioritize operational risk mitigation over aggressive technology implementation timelines. 43% on CONNECT1 BRT) with a ~$3M strategy.;2025-09-03 00:00:00+00;Funding;https://wtmj.com/news/2025/09/03/mcts-shares-progress-on-budget-deficit-addresses-fare-evasion-plans/;620WTMJ;https://wtmj.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/fuentes-and-kellner-2-9-3-25-e1756925853704-750x375.jpg;Adam Roberts;;"

The Milwaukee County Transit System (MCTS) is addressing a newly reduced budget deficit of $9.2 million and planning significant investments to combat revenue losses from fare evasion. With a system-wide evasion rate of 33%—and 43% on its CONNECT1 Bus Rapid Transit line—the agency is pursuing a nearly $3 million strategy that includes new onboard fare validation technology and a transition to open-loop payments by 2026.

column1: Addressing Revenue Leakage Through Modernized Fare Collection
At a Milwaukee County Committee on Transportation and Transit meeting
column2: MCTS leadership
column3: including new President and CEO Steve Fuentes
column4: detailed progress on lowering its ongoing budget deficit from $10.9 million to $9.2 million. A central focus of the discussion was a multi-faceted plan to address fare evasion
column5: which cost the agency approximately $4 million in 2024. The system-wide evasion rate was estimated at 33%
column6: with the CONNECT1 Bus Rapid Transit line experiencing a 43% evasion rate as of May.

column1: Officials estimate it will cost nearly $3 million to implement the new fare evasion strategy. This includes $1.1 million for additional uniformed security staff
column2: $1.2 million for fare ambassadors
column3: and <strong class=""font-semibold text-gray-900"">$302
column4: 000 for new onboard fare validation equipment. The agency's goal is to reduce fare evasion to 30% system-wide by September of 2026
column5: which is estimated to bring in <strong class=""font-semibold text-gray-900"">$900
column6: 000 in additional annual revenue.

column1: A key technical challenge was noted by former interim President and CEO Sandra Kellner
column2: who stated that implementing any new on-board fare evasion prevention technology aboard CONNECT1 buses would require discussion with the bus manufacturer to ensure the vehicle warranty is not impacted. Further technology enhancements include plans for open loop payment options by 2026
column3: allowing credit/debit cards and Apple/Google Pay. These new payment methods would honor the existing fare capping policies. Separately
column4: MCTS is set to receive roughly $8 million in federal Congestion Mitigation and Air Quality (CMAQ) Improvement Program funding
column5: which will be used for bus replacements and to explore traffic signal optimization.

column1: 4c3cc6cb-a681-46f4-856a-e566484cdd70;What is Paratransit Really Like?;paratransit-reality;Paratransit promises door-to-door service for disabled riders
column2:
but offers shared vans with 30+ minute waits and inflexible scheduling—exposing gaps in outdated ADA accessibility rules.;2025-10-28 00:00:00+00;;https://my.lwv.org/missouri/metro-st-louis/article/what-paratransit-really;League of Women Voters® of Metro St. Louis;https://my.lwv.org/sites/default/files/metro-st-louis/files/callaridestl.png;Barbara Sheinbein;;"

One of the LWVSTL positions is supporting local Paratransit for people with disabilities. I thought I would provide an overview of what it is really like
column3: since many in the public do not really understand. This can affect voting if a ride is needed to get to a polling location.

column2: I am sharing part of an email from Robyn Wallen
column3: Transportation Chair of the Missouri Council of the Blind. She writes:

column1: I am going to share some explanations on what different terms mean regarding Paratransit. This is an explanation of Door-to-Door service….A lot of people hear the phrase “door-to-door service” and assume paratransit takes you straight from your home to your destination—like Uber or medical transport. But here’s the reality: It’s a shared ride
column2: like a bus. You may stop multiple times for other passengers. “Door-to-door” often just means the driver can help you from your door to the van—but only if conditions allow. It doesn’t mean the driver will knock
column3: escort you inside
column4: or take you directly where you’re going. It doesn’t mean the ride will be fast or flexible. You may wait 30+ minutes before pickup and spend over an hour on the van. The term sounds helpful—but it hides how limited and inconsistent the service often is. Paratransit is a right under the ADA
column5: but it’s not a luxury. And “door-to-door” doesn’t mean what most people think it means. Understanding the difference matters because public assumptions shape funding
column6: policies
column7: and empathy.

column1: The Reality of Scheduling a Ride
I will add a bit more. You have to reserve your ride starting up to three days prior to the date you wish to travel. Although some communities have the same day reservations
column2: Metro does not at this time. A time slot may or may not be available for the time you wish to travel. If a time slot is not available
column3: you then get to choose a time slot which likely increases your trip time greatly or cancel your appointment or activity. Many riders schedule the pickup for an appointment an hour or so prior to when they actually have the appointment to be sure they are there on time. This can result in a long wait at your appointment location for your appointment and the same for a return trip. It can be tough to schedule more than one activity per day with all the built-in and unknown factors.

column1: When you get an appointment time
column2: you are not able to know the actual travel time. This is due to the fact that other riders are scheduling trips
column3: and the Call-a-Ride vehicle routing software puts the final runs for the drivers together on the morning for that date.

column1: A Necessary but Imperfect System

column2: having Paratransit is better than when it did not exist. This is especially true for someone using a wheelchair or other equipment which requires an accessible vehicle. These individuals cannot ride in most vehicles
column3: even a family or friend's vehicle. However
column4: it is not for someone who cannot or is unwilling to give a lot of extra time for their transportation.

column1: Many people with disabilities really do not have a choice but to use Paratransit either due to the need for an accessible vehicle or because they cannot afford other options. Most people just get in their car and go when they need or want to travel. Their schedule can be either determined in advance or at the last minute
column2: and they can be very flexible.

column1: Metro's Paratransit has actually improved in the past couple of years
column2: but it still has built-in issues. Part of the problem is that the law in the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) was written in 1990. Think of all the ways technology has changed in the last 35 years. If written today
column3:
the Paratransit system might be very different.

column5: they will provide the County with significant savings in fuel.

column1: Latimer said: “My administration is committed to reducing pollution and providing a greener future for all residents. 845cae82-0056-4895-a5e0-b3be42663390;Metro’s $4 million microtransit pilot has gone off the rails | Opinion;metro-microtransit-ballooned;"Evolve Houston has ballooned into a multimillion-dollar operation with little transparency and no proof it’s working.

;2025-10-23 00:00:00+00;;https://www.houstonchronicle.com/opinion/outlook/article/metro-microtransit-houston-lack-of-transparency-21114431.php;Houston Chronicle;https://s.hdnux.com/photos/01/50/02/22/27272377/5/960x0.webp;Charles Blain;;

Metro’s spending on the Evolve Houston microtransit program is about to cross the $4 million line
column2: and it is time for Houstonians — and City Council — to start asking some serious questions.

column1: Most Houstonians have little idea of this program because it serves so few people and only in certain neighborhoods. It involves little electric vehicles that seat five people. Riders use an Uber-like app to request a door-to-door ride.
Program Origins and Spiraling Costs
Evolve was originally pitched as a pilot program to serve communities where a significant portion of residents didn’t have a car. Technically
column2: Evolve is a nonprofit that pays for Ryde electric shuttles and funded by the City of Houston
column3: which is then reimbursed by Metro. The current Metro board chair
column4: Elizabeth Brock
column5: was a founding member and chair of Evolve’s board. Since the microtransit program began in 2023
column6: it has spiraled in size and cost
column7: providing free rides for key Inner Loop neighborhoods at a taxpayer expense without any rigorous measure of whether we are getting our money’s worth.

column2: a <strong class=""font-semibold text-gray-900"">six-month $216
column3: 000 extension of the test case quickly ballooned to a $1 million-plus initiative. Now the METRO committee overseeing this operation is considering another extension — more than $2.24 million to keep the program going and possibly growing until Sept. 2026.

column1: Don’t expect it to stop there. Watch the discussion at last week’s committee meeting
column2: and you’ll see interim Metro President and CEO Thomas Jasien casually mentioning the idea of adding even more money to the program for an ""enhanced"" dollar amount.

column1: A Pilot without Proof

Investing additional taxpayer dollars might make sense if this pilot were a proven success. But right now the board is still waiting on an update from an ongoing Ernst & Young assessment of the program. Board member Holly Vilaseca even asked when they would have that information in hand. She was told it would be another three weeks. What’s the point of having an assessment of Evolve if Metro isn’t going to use it to guide its decision-making? Because right now
column2: Metro is on path to spend $4.2 million on a program it still calls a “pilot.”

column1: Maybe Metro leadership is worried that the Ernst & Young report won’t deliver a positive assessment. It wouldn’t be the first red flag on the program. I recently filed an open records request about Evolve Houston and received an internal audit that highlighted how Evolve’s prices were “unsupportable.” The audit also pointed out that Evolve’s electric shuttles failed to meet federal requirements that vehicles be accessible for riders in wheelchairs.
That bad news shouldn’t be a surprise. Evolve has had problems from the very beginning.
Procurement Loopholes and Program Expansion
The initial pilot used a loophole to avoid state requirements for competitive bidding on large contracts. At the time
column2: the city argued that no other vendor could provide Evolve’s microtransit service. Each amendment since then has been an extension under that initial agenda item. And I would argue that the initial justification isn’t even accurate. In 2023
column3: Texas Department of Transportation identified a number of microtransit operations across the state.

column1: Microtransit pilots in other cities run up to 12 months to catch data during different seasons. We’re going on three years. At this point
column2: it is fair to say that Evolve is not a pilot program. It is an entire system built without ever having to go through formal procurement. Metro’s own presentations tout how they have expanded a “2-vehicle Evolve pilot to a 5-zone METRO microtransit system.” A system without wheelchair-accessible vehicles or air conditioning
column3: but a system nonetheless.

column1: An Uncertain Future

Maybe Evolve Houston is a worthwhile program. Maybe future Houstonians will view today’s Metro board as pioneers of a new and effective transit paradigm — even though I’d argue our local leaders should still leave the pioneering to the free market.

But my guess is that this will end up being a failed experiment that funnelled $4 million to a politically connected vendor that overcharged taxpayers for the privilege of free rides in glorified golf carts
column2: landing another blow to a transit agency already struggling with its public image.

column1: If Metro won’t be willingly transparent about Evolve Houston
column2: then it falls on City Hall to start demanding answers.
a frequent contributor to the Houston Chronicle
column3: researches and writes about policies that promote opportunity and social mobility.

column1: Around 40 percent of Milwaukee residents do not drive. More than 25 million rode the bus in 2024. The MCTS has seen an over 40 percent increase in ridership in the past five years but remains below pre-pandemic levels.

The Milwaukee County Transit System has relied on $192 million in federal American Rescue Plan Act funds since 2020. The transit system used $20 million in ARPA funds in 2025 and will use another $9 million in ARPA funds next year. But the transit system faces a $14 million budget deficit in 2026 when federal pandemic relief aid runs out.
Some on the board say they were blindsided because they learned about the deficit through the media rather than MCTS directly.
“We thought that they were operating at least whole or at a surplus

Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley presented his proposed 2026 budget to the county board. It called for eliminating six bus routes
column3: modifying five routes and increasing bus fares from $2 to $2.75 for adults. It would also discontinue the Transit Plus Same Day pilot program — a van service providing individuals with disabilities $10 rides for “vital trips” that cannot be scheduled the previous day.

column1: **ATLANTA**\- MARTA was awarded this week a $2.6 million grant by the U.S. Department of Transportation for the purchase of six zero-emission battery electric buses to replace six diesel models. Removing the 2005 diesel versions from the fleet will reduce MARTA’s emissions by 935 short tons of greenhouse gases and reduce energy consumption by 14.3 terajoules.

The energy saved by the deployment of the zero-emissions buses is equivalent to nearly 250 years of gas for the average American car driver. Zero-emissions vehicles also prevent the release of fine particulate matter – atmospheric pollutants suspended in the air in the form of minute solid particles or liquid droplets that can be hazardous to health and the environment.
“Receiving the Low-No grant will help MARTA put technologically advanced buses with low-to-no emission in densely populated areas with high ridership routes
column2: ” said MARTA General Manager and CEO Jeffrey Parker. “This welcomed first step toward making our bus fleet environmentally friendly will have community-wide impact. We are grateful to the Center for Transportation and the Environment (CTE) for their partnership and support in the application process and as we plan for installation and ongoing support of the fleet.”

column1: CTE is a nonprofit organization focused on the advancement of clean
column2: sustainable
column3: innovative transportation and energy technologies. Since its inception in 1993
column4: the organization has helped more than 200 companies improve their transportation technologies and fuels to reduce environmental impact.

column1: “We are excited to partner with MARTA on its first zero-emission bus deployment
column2: ” said Jason Hanlin
column3: CTE’s director of Technology Development. “CTE has helped more than 60 transit agencies across the country deploy zero-emission buses
column4: and we are especially proud to be working with our hometown agency in its critical efforts to advance sustainable transportation for the Atlanta region.”

The first Transporting Students with Disabilities and Special Needs Conference and Trade Show (TSD) was held in 1992. It was originally called the Transporting Students with Disabilities and Preschoolers National Conference. School Transportation News acquired the TSD Conference in 2012 and has faithfully continued it as the ultimate platform for addressing school transportation topics of importance pertaining to children with disabilities and special needs. Alternative school transportation is one such topic that has received widespread national attention recently.

column1: Both the National Association for Pupil Transportation (NAPT) and the National Association of State Directors of Pupil Transportation Services (NASDPTS) have recently written comprehensive industry papers on the topic.

The theme in both these publications is that school-age students should be transported in the yellow school bus to provide the highest level of school transportation safety. While this may not always be achievable or realistic
column2: depending on child-specific circumstances
column3: these associations acknowledge alternative transportation is a feasible option when the yellow bus is not the appropriate option.

column1: Advocating for National Standards

The annual TSD Conference has traditionally provided a setting for promoting a national dialogue on topics to move forward the agenda of safe school transportation for children with disabilities and special needs. From its inception in 1992
column2: when I was a keynote presenter

column4: promoting meaningful decision-making for this vulnerable population includes not compromising safety for cost-savings
column5: while simultaneously endorsing efficiency as a key component of the decision-making process.

column1: The 2022 TSD conference once again provided me a chance to compellingly advocate for the provision of safe transportation of children with disabilities and special needs in all modes of school transportation. My specific agenda at this meeting was to gain national attention for alternative transportation and its role in the provision of safe transportation
column2: when the “Yellow School Bus
column3: ” was eliminated as the most feasible mode of safe school transportation. My ultimate goal was that the 17th National Congress on Student Transportation (NCST) this past May would address alternative transportation and acknowledge its role in school transportation as a necessity for specific populations
column4: including students with disabilities and special needs.

column1: A Milestone at the National Congress on Student Transportation
It was rewarding when
column2: for the first time since the inception of this industry standard-setting exercise in 1939

column4: education associate for school transportation at the Delaware Department of Education.

The alternative transportation committee approval was a milestone as the first non-yellow school bus section addressed at the NCST and was a definitive victory for the well-being of children with disabilities and special needs.
Policy Recommendations and Best Practices
As an alternative transportation committee member
column2: I was committed to reinforcing that students with disabilities and special needs would only be recommended to receive alternative transportation services student’s individual needs. When assigning alternative transportation

column5: drivers

column1: IEP Team Involvement and Parent Communication
It recommends IEP teams
column2: including the parent and transportation personnel
column3: should be involved in discussing the mode of transportation for each individual child. The parent of the child with a disability should be made aware of the vehicle selection to provide their child’s related service transportation if a vehicle other than a school bus is used. When a school vehicle selection is changed during the course of a school year
column4: parents should be informed about the change. This change should be consistent with the current IEP approval for transportation services.

column1: Justifying and Evaluating Alternative Transportation

column2: it recommends annual IEP meetings should evaluate whether alternative transportation is required or whether the student can now be serviced by the school bus. The decision to utilize alternative transportation should never be a unilateral decision. It should only be recommended and approved through the IEP meeting process with oral and written justification for its necessity. Key considerations for endorsing alternative transportation should include the age of the child
column3: the impact of a child’s disability on providing safe transportation services in the selected alternative transportation vehicle
column4: the necessity for a reduction of length of ride time
column5: and the ability to provide safe access to a free appropriate public education (FAPE) within and outside of the school district.

column1: It is essential to consider what alternative transportation vehicle works best to ensure safe travel
column2: including the need for an attendant on the alternative transportation vehicle. This list is not exhaustive
column3: but other considerations include child safety restraint systems
column4: wheelchair transport and appropriate behavioral interventions.

column1: The low carbon fleet program focuses on four principles:

column1: # Solaris to rejuvenate electric buses in service with battery replacements
**Solaris is introducing a new service to keep ageing electric buses of its early customers up and running. Under the label 'Zero-emission vehicle retrofit
column2: ' the Polish manufacturer now offers the replacement of specific components—primarily batteries. The initiative is meant to prevent older electric buses from being decommissioned.**

column1: ![Solaris urbino electric elektrobus electric bus mpk krakau polen mpk krakow poland min](https://www.electrive.com/media/2025/12/solaris-urbino-18-electric-elektrobus-electric-bus-mpk-krakau-polen-mpk-krakow-poland-2025-01-min-1400x933.jpeg)
Solaris says it wants to offer its customers with earlier-generations of electric buses an effective solution to ensure these vehicles remain competitive in the public transport sector. To this end
column2: the company is turning the replacement of degraded batteries and other components into a standardised service offering. Owners of the electric buses thus have the option to retrofit
column3: i.e. the ‘post-warranty replacement of EV components with new ones’
column4: according to the manufacturer. The new service applies not only to battery-electric buses
column5: but also to battery-trolleybuses and
column6: in future
column7: to Solaris’ hydrogen buses.

column1: Solaris has been producing electric buses since 2011
column2: and according to the company
column3: several thousand of its vehicles are now in operation across Europe— ‘some successfully serving passengers for over a decade and covering nearly a million kilometres’
column4: the company noted. Replacing batteries and other components after the warranty period is designed to help operators ‘in maximising the return on their e-mobility investments’.

column1: Solaris is not the only manufacturer addressing how to support early customers whose electric buses are no longer performing as well. On the one hand
column2: battery capacity naturally degrades over time; on the other
column3: rapid technological advancements in recent years have led to significantly improved and more efficient technology platforms in newer-generation electric buses. Daimler Buses has already introduced [comprehensive battery services for the eCitaro](https://www.electrive.com/2025/04/11/daimler-buses-sets-up-comprehensive-battery-services-for-ecitaro/) this spring and will offer upgrades to the latest battery generation for older vehicles from 2026 onwards.

column1: 78112001	Mixed mode urban and suburban transportation for passengers

The Florida Department of Transportation is announcing the availability of funding to support intercity bus service in Florida. Intercity bus service is defined as regularly scheduled bus service for the general public that operates with limited stops over fixed routes connecting two or more urban areas not in close proximity. The service must make meaningful connections with scheduled intercity bus service to more distant points
column2: if such service is available. The provider must also maintain schedule information in the National Official Bus Guide and provide package express service incidental to passenger transportation.

column1: Rocky labor relationship
SEPTA is known as one of the most strike-prone large transit systems in the country. Union workers have walked off the job at least 12 times since 1975.
Will there be a strike this time? It’s probably too early to say.
But TWU Local 234’s early move to avoid a strike authorization vote is a conciliatory gesture. It may be possible to reach a deal with less of the usual brinkmanship.
Said Vera: “I have hope.”

k. Network interfaces between ATS-B and the Crosstown Wayside Communication Network/5G DCS;
l. Enhancements to the ATS-B Graphical User Interface for improved emergency braking incident diagnostics;
m. Smart Power Distribution Units in ATS-B and Wayside Communication Network Cabinets;
n. New ATS-B command to release switches and overruns;
o. Standardized Local ATS and Line ATS systems for CBTC-centric projects; and
p. Addition of static and dynamic indicators to Human-Machine Interface ATS-B Displays for bridges

ST. LOUIS — Saint Louis Public Schools has announced a new transportation partner that will start this summer.
It comes with a $30 million price tag but also promises safety and consistency.
The new company is called [Zum](https://www.ridezum.com/) and is used by schools in places like Kansas City and Omaha.
The school district said they will only need this one vendor to meet all of their transportation needs compared to the 18 they use now.
In the latest update at the St. Louis Public School Board meeting
column2: Chief Operations Officer Square Watson said the on-time percentage of getting students to and from school is currently less than 15%.

column1: Watson said the solution to transportation troubles Zum.
“They had a 98 percent on-time performance rate. They also had zero school bus driver shortages
column2: ” Watson said to the board.

column1: In the contract with the district
column2: they will provide 220 buses
column3: 55 of which will be equipped to serve students with special needs. There's potential for some of them to be electric
column4: all of them will have air conditioning and parents along with the schools will be able to track where students are.

column1: “All of the drivers were equipped with the iPad and one of the key areas was ridership. So each student had an RFID card. So in other words
column2: as they were entering the bus
column3: they would be able to use the card and we would be able to really accurately track ridership
column4: ” Watson said.

column2: the drivers will have competitive wages and benefits.

column1: “They had some rewards and things that incentivize their drivers almost on a monthly basis. So the idea and goal even within their proposal is that they are going to provide a more state-of-the-art facility and improve their facility
column2: ” Watson said.

column3: through June 30

column1: “I wanted to recognize and commend you all because I think in the three to four years I've been on the board

This report presents early results from a deployment of fuel cell electric buses (FCEBs) operated by Orange County Transportation Authority (OCTA) in Southern California. The ten FCEBs
column2: produced by New Flyer
column3: feature an electric drive propulsion system powered by a Ballard fuel cell system. The project team is collaborating with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and DOE's National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) to evaluate the buses in revenue service. The goal of this evaluation is to compare the FCEB performance to that of conventional technology and to track progress over time toward meeting the technical targets set by DOE and the Department of Transportation (DOT). The FCEBs were delivered beginning in late-2019
column4: and were placed in service on February 9
column5: 2020. The data period covers February 2020 through July 2021. NREL collects data on ten 2016 model year compressed natural gas (CNG) buses as a baseline comparison at OCTA.

column1: The Wasatch Front is one of the fastest-growing regions in the nation. UTA is helping guide this growth by providing services that improve air quality
column2: conserve our natural resources
column3: and enhance our quality of life.

column2: including 15 that serve the Ogden Express (OGX) bus rapid transit (BRT) line. The remaining buses operate in Salt Lake County
column3: frequently serving Routes 2
column5: and 509
column6: which are well supported by electric charging infrastructure.

column2: Midvalley Express (April 2026) and Davis-Salt Lake City Community Connector (April 2028)
column3: will operate entirely with battery-electric buses.

column1: UTA’s Tooele County UTA On Demand zone operates with 10 Ford E-Transit electric
column2: ADA-accessible
column3: 6-passenger vans. The service is supported by electric chargers in the system and overnight electric chargers at the zone garage.

column1: The [UTA Zero-Emission Bus Transition Plan](https://www.rideuta.com/-/media/Files/About-UTA/Innovative-Mobility/2023_UTA_Zero_Emission_Bus_Transition_Plan.pdf) is a comprehensive approach to help lower UTA fleet emissions through 2040
column2: a key to improving Utahns’ quality of life through cleaner air.

electric buses comprise approximately 8% of the UTA bus fleet. The transition plan target is 50% alternative fuel vehicles in the fleet by 2040: 36% (or nearly 200 vehicles) powered by electric batteries and 14% fueled by compressed natural gas.

column1: The transition plan includes the development of electric charging infrastructure throughout the UTA service network. [This map](https://www.rideuta.com/-/media/Files/About-UTA/Innovative-Mobility/On_Route_Chargers_map.pdf)  shows current
column2: planned
column3: and potential 300 kW overhead pantograph chargers located strategically in the UTA network for on-route charging. Overnight 150 kW chargers are located at regional bus maintenance facilities. UTA schedules sequential hour-long charging periods during the night to mitigate electrical grid impact.

column1: The Low or No Emission Grant Program (49 U.S.C. 5339(c)) and the Grants for Buses and Bus Facilities Competitive Program (5339(b)) administered by the Federal Transit Administration are major funding sources for this transition plan. The plan includes all the information required to apply for these grants. Additional funding for electric buses may come from the Environmental Protection Agency through the Beehive Emissions Reduction Plan.
**Air Quality Monitoring Project With Live Data Feed**

column2: three TRAX lines have been equipped with research-grade monitors that cover the valley
column3: relaying emissions data every 10 seconds. This project was implemented in partnership with [Salt Lake County](https://www.saltlakecounty.gov/health/air-quality/)
column4: the University of Utah
column5: and the Utah Department of Environmental Quality. Visit the [project page](https://atmos.utah.edu/air-quality/trax/index.php) for additional information.

column2: air quality monitors were added to some of UTA’s electric buses. As of 2025
column3: 15 electric buses have been equipped with monitors
column4: detecting PM2.5
column5: PM10
column6: and ozone. This project has allowed for more flexible data collection. Additionally
column7: it provides essential information for future air quality improvement efforts and ensures that communities with the worst air quality receive attention. Visit the [live data feed](https://utahaq.chpc.utah.edu/) for more details.

column1: UTA and Rocky Mountain Power (RMP) enjoy a collaborative partnership in pursuit of joint clean energy projects. This partnership focuses on energy efficiency
column2: electric vehicles
column3: electrical infrastructure
column4: grid resilience
column5: and research and grants.

column2: UTA participates in RMP’s Blue Sky program by buying Renewable Energy Certificates to support the green energy supply to the grid. UTA also supports public charging infrastructure at transit park and ride locations like 3900 South Wasatch Boulevard.
see [UTA’s WattSmart Plan](https://www.rideuta.com/-/media/Files/About-UTA/Innovative-Mobility/Innovation/Wattsmart_UTA_Energy_Plan_Final.pdf).

column1: UTA is part of the steering committee for Utah’s [electrified transportation action plan](https://le.utah.gov/interim/2024/pdf/00003612.pdf). This collaborative effort builds on the research and development expertise of Advancing Sustainability through Powered Infrastructure for Roadway Electrification (ASPIRE) at Utah State University
column2: as well as partnerships with industry and business across the state.

column1: ASPIRE received $2.1 million in ongoing funding through Senate Bill 125 Transportation Infrastructure Amendments
column2: signed into law by Governor Spencer Cox in March 2023. With this new ongoing funding
column3: ASPIRE will facilitate strategic planning and development to guide Utah to an electrified and intelligent transit system.

column1: In connection with this regional effort
column2: UTA and USU researchers led the development of an energy management system for UTA’s intermodal hub at Salt Lake Central Station. The system mitigates [peak load impacts](https://www.rideuta.com/-/media/Images/About-UTA/Innovative-Mobility-2022/Bus_Grid_Impact2025.png) by momentarily shutting off electric bus fast chargers when TRAX vehicles depart from Salt Lake Central Station. The project's final report can be found [here](https://www.rideuta.com/-/media/Files/About-UTA/Innovative-Mobility/323798RMPAtt10Exhibit17A4_29_2022_intermoal_hub_project_final_report.pdf).

column1: # RideCo Partners with New York MTA to Implement the Largest Modernized Paratransit Service in North America

column2: NY
column3: October 21
column4: 2025 — RideCo
column5: the on-demand transit technology leader serving 5 of the 10 largest cities in the U.S.
column6: announced today it has been awarded a contract with MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) to adopt its industry-leading
column7: cloud-based dynamic transit software for its Access-A-Ride paratransit service. NYCT operates the largest paratransit service in North America with a fleet of more than 1
column8: 100 dedicated vehicles and a network of thousands of taxis
column9: TNC
column10: and for-hire vehicle providers that deliver nearly 1 million rides monthly.**

column1: RideCo is excited for this opportunity to partner with the Access-A-Ride team to build the next generation of service for New York
column2: building on NYCT’s focus on modernizing their service to meet the growing
column3: changing needs of riders. RideCo’s selection for this historic contract underscores the company’s demonstrated ability to deliver successful outcomes with large-scale fleets
column4: and to deliver continuous service improvements.

column1: “The commitment of New York City Transit and the Access-A-Ride team to innovation and world-class excellence is inspiring. They have already made significant strides in improving service
column2: and we’re excited to help take that progress to the next level
column3: ” said Prem Gururajan
column4: Co-Founder and CEO
column5: RideCo. “We are thrilled to partner with New York City Transit to deliver modernized technology infrastructure
column6: one that continuously optimizes
column7: streamlines operations for greater efficiency
column8: and enhances the rider experience. Together with NYCT
column9: we look forward to making one of the world's great cities

column2: continuous optimization technology represents a major opportunity for New York City. By streamlining and automating elements of the eligibility
column3: reservations
column4: scheduling
column5: and same day dispatching functions
column6: RideCo is excited to help NYCT sustain and scale its growing
column7: mission critical service
column8: while also improving the rider experience. The modernized technology system will rollout in phases in 2026 and 2027.

column1: **RideCo is the transit technology leader serving 5 of the 10 largest cities in the U.S.** Trusted by transit agencies
column2: as well as fleet operators and technology companies
column3: the platform powers the largest on-demand transit services in North America including Philadelphia
column4: Houston and San Antonio. Scores of others have also adopted RideCo’s transformative software
column5: rooted in proprietary continuous optimization technology
column6: and supported by the industry’s best customer service.

column1: From reducing overall travel time and encouraging sustainable public transit usage to improving operational efficiency and delivering the lowest total cost of ownership
column2: RideCo’s dynamic transit technology is advancing transit for riders and reshaping how transit agencies operate their services. To date
column3: RideCo has been awarded five U.S. patents

column1: ### **RFI: DC MID - General Call for Projects**
The DC MID sought innovative solutions and support from private-sector partners to address key mobility challenges in DC. Through this Request for Information (RFI)
column2: the DC MID aimed to identify viable solutions and non-financial support needed to demonstrate cutting-edge transportation technologies
column3: business models
column4: and infrastructure improvements. Focus areas included first/last-mile transportation
column5: e-mobility charging
column6: safe streets
column7: event congestion
column8: and curb management.

column1: The electrification of the transportation sector was rapidly accelerating
column2: driven by growing consumer demand
column3: increasing affordability
column4: and government incentives aimed at encouraging the industry and consumers to address the climate crisis. Despite rising investments
column5: access to charging infrastructure remained a significant barrier for many people considering the purchase of an electric vehicle
column6: especially in dense environments such as SWDC
column7: where at-home charging was simply not an option for many residents.

column1: One of the core challenges in advancing Universal Basic Mobility (UBM) was developing an integrated financial platform that could connect multiple funding sources and facilitate payments to a range of vendors. Ideally
column2: such a platform would not only have supported UBM benefits but also enabled employers
column3: developers
column4: and individuals to load
column5: store
column6: and replenish value for use across various transportation services—while also facilitating trip discovery and planning. In recognition that transportation mobility was foundational to economic mobility
column7: the MID prepared to launch a 12-month UBM demonstration project that would provide residents with a direct transportation subsidy. To support this initiative
column8: the MID issued a new Call for Projects to identify a partner capable of disbursing these funds through a flexible
column9: user-centered platform.

column1: The goal of the Call for Projects was to seek innovative solutions that could improve the mobility ecosystem in the District of Columbia. Where the DCMID felt that proposed solutions fit the established needs
column2: it contacted vendors to formally discuss potential projects and negotiate contractual agreements. This Call for Projects was not a formal solicitation
column3: and the DCMID was under no obligation to select any of the vendors who responded to the request.

column1: # METRO Rolls Out Electric Buses on Two Local Routes

![METRO Electric Bus charging at the bus depot.](https://assets-cdn-prod.azureedge.us/assets/images/default-source/newsletters/metro-connections-images/2024/august/electric-bus-charging.jpg?sfvrsn=b78dc0c_1)

column2: passengers on the 402 Bellaire Quickline and the 28 OST - Wayside are experiencing a quieter
column3: smoother ride during their commute thanks to METRO's rollout of zero-emission electric buses.

column1: As part of the service change
column2: the 402 Bellaire Quickline transitioned to an all-electric fleet while the 28 OST - Wayside has five electric buses. The pilot project represents a significant step toward enhancing customer experience
column3: improving service reliability
column4: and reducing environmental impact.

column1: The 402 Bellaire Quickline runs from Chinatown to the Medical Center
column2: serving Southwest Houston
column3: Gulfton
column4: and the City of Bellaire. The 28 OST-Wayside route serves neighborhoods like Fifth Ward
column5: which has historically faced air pollution challenges.

column1: “These improvements are an investment in our customers. The new electric buses meet our broader goals of ensuring the system is accessible
column2: equitable
column3: and helps us close environmental justice gaps
column4: ” said METRO Board Chair Elizabeth Gonzalez Brock. “Both routes service some communities that include minority
column5: low-income
column6: and transit-dependent populations. It is so important we provide these customers with an easy and safe choice that they will be excited to ride.”

column1: In addition to the environmental benefits
column2: the buses offer enhanced features
column3: including USB charging ports
column4: three wheelchair securement areas
column5: and more space for ADA patrons—upgrades from the aging fleets serving both routes.

column1: ![Andrei Dragomir deomstrates features of METRO's new electric bus.](https://assets-cdn-prod.azureedge.us/assets/images/default-source/newsletters/metro-connections-images/2024/august/andrei.jpg?sfvrsn=ade4d31b_1)
**_Sustainable Transit:_** _Andrei Dragomir gives a tour of an electric bus._
METRO’s Director of Technical Services Andrei Dragomir oversaw the procurement and rigorous testing of the new buses
column2: which began in June 2024.

column1: “We put the buses out there empty first. We ran them on the route to check for defects
column2: performance
column3: and range
column4: ” said Dragomir. “After we found no issues
column5: we decided to test them with passengers.”

column1: The selection of these routes was based on several factors
column2: including the populations served
column3: potential charging locations
column4: and whether all or most buses on the route could be electric.

column1: Moving away from diesel is a key component of the Authority’s initiative to build a more sustainable and efficient transit system.
While the agency already has more than 400 clean-running diesel-electric hybrid buses
column2: a Federal Transit Administration (FTA) grant will ensure METRO has 20 electric buses and 14 hydrogen fuel cell electric buses in total in the coming years.

column1: Dragomir anticipates the first hydrogen fuel cell buses arriving in late 2025 or early 2026. The buses will also undergo similar testing to determine the most suitable routes and necessary infrastructure to support them.

![Rear electrical components of a METRO electric bus.](https://assets-cdn-prod.azureedge.us/assets/images/default-source/newsletters/metro-connections-images/2024/august/electric-bus-rear.jpg?sfvrsn=252a3164_1)

column1: # Singapore’s LTA looks to the future with cloud-based fleet management technology from Trapeze Group

**The world-leading public transport services delivered by Singapore’s Land Transport Authority (LTA) are set to be enhanced by a cloud-based Bus Fleet Management System (BFMS) implemented by the Trapeze Group-ST Engineering consortium.**
The consortium successfully implemented a Centralised Fleet Management System that has supported Singapore’s bus fleet since 2014 and currently oversees about 5
column2: 800 buses across four public transport operators. Under the new agreement
column3: the consortium is due to transition to the next-generation franchising solution. Trapeze’s latest fleet management software and in-vehicle hardware enables LTA and their operators to continue delivering connected and reliable journeys for Singapore’s residents and tourists

column2: Trapeze Group ASEAN Managing Director
column3: said Trapeze’s experience as the established ITS technology provider to LTA can streamline the Bus Fleet Management System implementation.

column1: “I am delighted LTA can continue its long-term relationship with the Trapeze Group-ST Engineering consortium
column2: which powers outstanding public transport experiences for people living in or visiting Singapore
column3: ” Mr Hesse said.

column1: _“Trapeze is proud of the high-level service and expertise we have provided to LTA over the past decade and I look forward to reaching new heights together in the years to come as we help LTA Singapore deliver its 2040 vision._”
Trapeze’s Intelligent Transport System (ITS) is the core component of LTA’s BFMS
column2: built upon a real-time cloud?based software architecture with open API’s and a new WebGUI front-end. By transferring LTA’s system to the Government Commercial Cloud
column3: the Trapeze solution can improve the productivity of bus service controllers while keeping a high level of cybersecurity.

column1: The new Trapeze system uses enhanced analytic functionalities to continuously optimise models and produce even more accurate bus arrival-time predictions. Trapeze’s Business Intelligence solution also enables business users to create reports on key outcomes in a more flexible and dynamic manner.
![A bright green bus drives through the streets of Singapour. ](https://www.it-trans.org/medien/medien/bilder/presse/aussteller-pressemitteilungen/singapore_02_1920x0.jpg)

column2: certified to industry-leading ITxPT specifications
column3: are due to be installed on all buses across Singapore’s network. This innovative hardware
column4: on a Linux OS platform
column5: sets a new standard in processing power
column6: data transmission rates
column7: and location accuracy for LTA. Crucially
column8: these improve interoperability between in-vehicle systems
column9: demonstrating LTA’s commitment to progressive and future-ready bus services.

column1: The solution also supports Singapore’s current EV bus fleet rollout with monitoring of EV assets through seamless integration with on-board technology and the charging infrastructure platform.

To learn more about Trapeze Group’s suite of ITS technology please visit our website [here](https://trapezegroup.com.my/public-transport-authorities/intelligent-transport-systems/) or [contact our team](https://trapezegroup.com.my/contact-us/) to get the conversation started.
